judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports fully and correctly answer all three questions with equivalent grounding; comparable overall.

report_1.evidence: The application connects to Postgres as **`cedar_owner`**, the role that owns the tables and is the cluster superuser — and Postgres does not enforce Row-Level Security against such a role... `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` only does `ALTER TABLE clients ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` — **not `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`**... `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`

report_2.evidence: The application connects to Postgres as **`cedar_owner`**, and `backend/app/db.py` builds its one engine straight from this `DATABASE_URL`... even a non-superuser table owner is exempt from RLS on its own tables unless `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` is set. `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` only does `ALTER TABLE clients ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` (no `FORCE`). So although the `tenant_isolation` policy... is correctly written... the policy is **never evaluated** for this connection

report_1.evidence: Make the application run as the dedicated, unprivileged `cedar_app` role instead of the owner: change the runtime `DATABASE_URL`... to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`... Keep ownership/DDL on `cedar_owner` and grant `cedar_app` only DML... a clear split between a migration/owner role and a runtime app role.

report_2.evidence: Change `DATABASE_URL`... to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`... it does not own the tables — so RLS policies *are* enforced for it... Keep the two-role separation permanently: use a schema-owning role (`cedar_owner`) **only** to run migrations... and the app role (`cedar_app`) for everything the app does at runtime.

report_1.evidence: The `cedar_app` role is a **cluster-global object** created only by the one-time bootstrap script `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which is explicitly *not* part of migrations... The reset script `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, whose `-v` deletes the `cedar_pgdata` volume... The reset script then only re-runs `./scripts/migrate.sh`... and never re-runs the bootstrap... the bootstrap must be re-run after every volume wipe

report_2.evidence: `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, and the `-v` flag **deletes that volume**, destroying all roles including `cedar_app`... nothing re-creates `cedar_app`. Creating it is a separate one-time bootstrap (`db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`)... that `reset-local-db.sh` never invokes... the one-time bootstrap must be re-run against the fresh cluster
